howItWorks.intro: This calculator breaks down the cost of each handloaded round from its individual components: primer, powder, bullet, and brass. It then compares your reloading cost to factory ammunition prices.
rules: Powder cost = (charge in grains ÷ 7,000) × price per pound
Bullet cost = box price ÷ bullets per box
Brass cost = price per case ÷ number of reloads before discard
howItWorks.outro: Total cost per round is the sum of all four components. The break-even calculation tells you how many rounds you need to reload before your equipment investment pays for itself through savings versus factory ammo.

faq.question: Is reloading ammunition cheaper than buying factory ammo?
faq.answer: In most cases, yes. Reloading typically saves 30-60% per round compared to factory ammo, depending on the caliber and components used. The savings are most significant for larger or premium calibers like .300 Win Mag or 6.5 Creedmoor.

faq.question: How many times can I reload brass?
faq.answer: Most rifle brass can be reloaded 5-10 times, and pistol brass can go 10-20+ times depending on the cartridge and pressure levels. Signs of worn brass include split necks, loose primer pockets, and head separation marks.

faq.question: How many grains of powder are in a pound?
faq.answer: There are 7,000 grains in one pound of powder. So a typical .308 Win load using 42 grains per round yields about 166 rounds per pound of powder.

faq.question: What equipment do I need to start reloading?
faq.answer: At minimum you need a reloading press, dies for your caliber, a powder scale, a powder measure, a case trimmer, and a reloading manual. A quality single-stage press setup typically costs $300-600. Progressive presses for higher volume cost $500-1,500.

faq.question: How long does it take to break even on reloading equipment?
faq.answer: With a $500 equipment investment and savings of $0.50 per round, you'd break even after reloading 1,000 rounds. Most active shooters recoup their investment within the first year. High-volume shooters break even much faster.

faq.question: Does this calculator include the cost of reloading time?
faq.answer: No, this calculator covers component costs only. An experienced reloader on a single-stage press produces about 50-100 rounds per hour. Progressive presses can produce 200-500+ rounds per hour. Whether your time factors in is a personal decision.

sections.title: What Is the Reloading Cost Calculator?
sections.body: The Reloading Cost Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your primer cost, powder cost, powder charge, you get instant results including cost per round, cost per round, cost per box of 20. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting cost per round right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your primer cost and need to find the right cost per round. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Primer Cost (per 1,000)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your primer cost — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: powder cost, powder charge, bullet cost, bullets per box, brass cost, brass reloads before discard, factory ammo price, equipment investment. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your cost per round, cost per round, cost per box of 20. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your primer cost before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
